nativeRange.habitat: The Niger Trigger inhabits current-swept outer reef slopes and channels, where it forms large aggregations in open water feeding on zooplankton. When threatened, individuals retreat into rubble patches, crevices, and caves. Juveniles tend to shelter closer to the substrate than adults.

diet.notes: In the wild, primarily a midwater planktivore feeding on zooplankton, supplemented with sponges. In captivity it readily accepts a varied meaty diet: krill, mysis, squid, clams on the half shell, chopped shrimp, small pieces of fish, and marine pellets. Hard-shelled foods such as whole shrimp or clams are generally advised to help wear down the species' continuously growing red incisor-like teeth.

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso, 6052x3944. Striking electric-blue adult in full lateral profile, sharp; shows the diagnostic long trailing caudal lobes, purple fin margins, blue facial lines and red teeth.
Diego Delso, 5071x2975. Vivid blue adult, sharp clean profile on plain background; deeply forked long caudal lobes and blue face lines clearly shown.
Diego Delso, 6351x4234. Blue-to-purple adult in a reef setting (different angle/background); trailing tail lobes and red teeth visible.

summary: The Niger Trigger is a purple-to-blue planktivorous triggerfish with distinctive red incisor-like teeth and long trailing tail lobes. Often considered one of the more peaceful members of the triggerfish family, it is generally regarded as reef-safe with caution regarding motile invertebrates.
careNotes:
The Niger Trigger (also widely sold as Red-Tooth Trigger or Redtooth Triggerfish, occasionally as Blue-Throat Niger) is commonly considered one of the more peaceful and well-mannered triggerfishes for the home aquarium. Coloration varies day-to-day across purple, blue and blue-green, with lighter blue highlights along the fins and long trailing caudal lobes; mature adults develop the distinctive bright red teeth that give the species its common name.

It is generally advised to provide a tank of at least 700 L (around 180-200 US gal) with a long footprint, plentiful live rock arranged into caves and overhangs, and substantial open swimming space. Because the species is a current-swept midwater planktivore in the wild, vigorous flow and vertical swimming room matter as much as floor area. They have been known to rearrange rockwork. A secure, well-weighted lid is generally advised - triggers are powerful and can displace loose covers.

Reef compatibility is debated. The Niger Trigger is often described as reef-safe-with-caution: it has been reported to generally leave corals alone but will readily eat ornamental shrimp, small crabs, snails and other small motile invertebrates. Individual personality varies; some specimens become more nippy or territorial as they mature, especially if underfed or kept in cramped quarters. Best kept as a single specimen with robust semi-aggressive tankmates of similar size; conspecific aggression is generally high outside very large systems.
breedingNotes: Not bred in home aquaria. In the wild the species is oviparous with distinct pair-bonding; eggs are laid in benthic nests and both parents have been reported to guard the site, though only the female tends the eggs.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary care reference. Provides species ID, family, captive water parameters (72-78 F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12), 180 gal minimum, aggressive temperament, not reef safe (LiveAquaria's conservative classification), carnivorous diet, easy care level.

additional.name: Reef Builders
additional.notes: Cross-check on temperament - characterizes the species as usually peaceful and well-mannered by triggerfish standards, with individual personality variation. Supports the semi-aggressive (not aggressive) classification used here.

additional.name: Top Shelf Aquatics - Triggerfish care guide
additional.notes: Cross-check on reef-safe-with-caution status: Niger Triggers are usually coral-safe but will consume motile invertebrates and shrimp; underfeeding and cramped tanks increase aggression.

fish.breedingNotes: The Niger Trigger has not been bred in captivity. In the wild, the species is oviparous, with bonded pairs depositing eggs in benthic nests and both parents reported to guard the site, though the female takes primary responsibility for tending the eggs.
